mechanism: A mixed-method study first identifies structural dimensions of a phenomenon through inductive analysis and develops predictive hypotheses. Experimental conditions are then randomly assigned to participants; outcome quantities are compared between treatment and control groups using analysis of variance to test for group differences. Qualitative text data from secondary sources is coded and thematically analyzed to verify alignment of patterns across methodologies. Two separate intervention conditions manipulate a predictor variable, and binary outcome improvement is measured to assess causality in a smaller sample.

An experiment asks observers to classify entities along an ordered scale and identify the decision boundary between two categories. The threshold location is extracted from response data. A second within-subjects experiment varies stimulus context systematically and measures how classification boundaries shift with timing constraints, revealing whether boundary computation depends on real-time vs. pre-computed reference values.

Observational comparison identifies differences in the frequency and automaticity of a class of intrusive thoughts in two populations. Free-association and word-pair association tasks measure changes in semantic network connectivity and interference with unrelated cognition. Mental effort required to suppress target thoughts is inferred from residual intrusion frequency.

Participants undergo a conditioning protocol that repeatedly pairs stimuli with an outcome; extinction training then decouples these stimuli from outcomes. At recall, multimodal measurements are collected: skin conductance (an indicator of autonomic response) and scalp electrical recordings are analyzed with source localization to estimate neural oscillatory power in anatomically defined regions. The differential response (treatment vs. control stimulus) is extracted from oscillatory signatures and correlated with behavioral suppression.

Participants perform a task individually at baseline, then are randomly assigned to three conditions that modify task context (isolated vs. interdependent teams, with and without peer communication). Task persistence time is measured at follow-up. A two-way analysis of variance with between-subjects factors (gender, condition) is computed on outcome changes. Secondary psychological measures are collected but treated similarly.
domain: exercise science behavior change and online groups

mechanism: Participants view an advertisement embedded in webpages that differ systematically on a visual property (background complexity). Attitudes toward the advertisement, brand, and website are measured via self-report. Mean attitudes are compared between background conditions using univariate statistical tests to assess whether the visual manipulation influences the outcome vector.

Survey data are collected from participants nested within institutions. Two types of aggregate predictors are constructed: composition of a population on a dimension, and the mean of individual behavioral engagement within that population. Multilevel regression is fitted with fixed effects for individual predictors and random intercepts for institution-level heterogeneity. The unique contribution of each predictor type to outcome variance is assessed, and cross-level interactions test whether the effect of composition is moderated by individual engagement.
domain: higher education diversity and student learning

mechanism: A large international survey dataset is obtained and merged with contextual institutional variables. Multilevel regression is fitted where individual outcome is regressed on individual- and school-level predictors, with random intercepts for schools and random slopes for a key individual predictor. Moderation is tested by including interactions between the individual predictor and school-level variables. The magnitude of a focal effect is assessed within subgroups defined by individual characteristics.

A nationally representative dataset links individual student characteristics to teacher judgments of cognitive ability. A three-level hierarchical linear model is fitted with students nested in classrooms nested in schools. The residual variance in teacher judgments after controlling for student characteristics is decomposed: part is explained by observed classroom demographics and resources, and the remainder is attributed to unmeasured teacher or context factors. Group-level disparities in judgments are compared to group-level disparities in measured ability.
domain: educational equity teacher expectations and assessment bias

mechanism: Longitudinal measurements of a quantity are collected at multiple time points for individuals in two groups. Piecewise linear regression is fitted with separate slope and intercept parameters for successive time intervals within each group. The functional form captures acceleration and deceleration in trajectory shape. Groups are compared on trajectory parameters to infer whether growth rates are identical or divergent and whether convergence occurs at any time point.

DNA sequence data from a sample of affected individuals is obtained via commercial genotyping. Variant frequencies in the sample are extracted and compared to a reference population to identify enriched variants. Pathway enrichment analysis identifies over-representation of genes with enriched variants in precompiled biological annotation databases. Computational scoring algorithms assign pathogenicity ranks to identified variants based on sequence conservation and structural properties.

Participants are randomly assigned to conditions that activate a semantic concept implicitly (e.g., via exposure to words or images). A second manipulation varies cognitive demand during the activation task. Participants then rate their judgments of scenarios on a rating scale. The outcome is regressed on the activation condition, the demand condition, and their interaction, using ordinary least squares to test moderation of the priming effect.

Participants are randomly assigned to two training conditions. Before and after training, they perform a stimulus-response task presented as video scenarios; speed and accuracy of decision are recorded along with eye movement patterns. Performance metrics are extracted from baseline and follow-up assessments. Between-condition differences on outcome change scores are evaluated using univariate statistical tests to assess training effectiveness.

Participants perform a categorization task where a target stimulus is presented together with flanking stimuli. Response times and accuracy are recorded. A congruency metric is computed: whether flankers match the target on a relevant dimension. Reaction time and error rates are compared between congruent and incongruent flanker conditions. A second experiment manipulates whether flankers and targets share an abstract property rather than sensory identity to dissociate perceptual from response-level interference.
domain: cognitive psychology selective attention and conflict

mechanism: A theoretical framework defines a metric for dissimilarity between entities as a weighted sum of feature differences. The model incorporates asymmetry (where role matters) and contrast effects (where salience of matching vs. differing features depends on context). Empirical predictions are derived: when a predictor increases feature salience, similarity judgments should change in predictable ways. Experiments test these predictions by manipulating context or stimulus properties and measuring how similarity ratings shift.

Two studies combine cross-sectional survey and daily diary data. In Study 1, employees' demographic and family structure characteristics are recorded, and work absorption (a composite of self-reported engagement) is measured. Structural groups are compared on mean absorption. In Study 2, employees report anticipated after-work activities daily; multilevel regression nests days within individuals and tests whether the relationship between family structure and absorption is mediated by anticipated activity count.
domain: organizational psychology work-life boundary and engagement

mechanism: A conceptual analysis examines how human causal attributions deviate from a normative standard. The framework proposes that causal judgments integrate two sources: a statistical relationship (whether manipulation of an event changes outcome probability) and a norm violation signal (whether the causal candidate deviates from expected behavior). The model then predicts which event in a sequence will be identified as the cause based on which event violates a norm, not merely which event is statistically sufficient.

A philosophical argument analyzes causal judgment as combining three components: event classification (recognizing what kind of entity an event is), norm violation detection (comparing event properties to normative expectations), and control attribution (assigning responsibility based on whether the agent deviated from norms). The framework unifies diverse phenomena in causal attribution and culpability assignment under a single mechanistic account.
